# Financial Deep Research: Vistra Corp (VST)

Research conducted: 2026-05-05
Stage 1 baseline provided: No — §9 will note the absence

## 1. Recent Financial Performance & Quarterly Trends

### Most Recent Report
**Most recent quarter:** Q4 2025, reported 2026-02-26. Q1 2026 results are scheduled for release on 2026-05-07 and were not available as of this research date.

For the three months ended December 31, 2025, Vistra reported net income of $233 million, down from $490 million in Q4 2024. Adjusted EBITDA from Ongoing Operations was $1.74 billion versus $1.98 billion in the prior-year period. GAAP earnings per share for the quarter was approximately $2.18–$2.34 depending on the source, missing consensus estimates that had ranged from $2.33 to $2.51. Revenue for the quarter totaled $4.58 billion, up 13.5% year-over-year but below consensus expectations of roughly $5.3–$5.75 billion. [Source: Company Q4 2025 Earnings Release / Title: Q4 and Full-Year 2025 Results / Date: 2026-02-26]***; [Source: Zacks / Title: "Vistra's Q4 Earnings Miss Estimates, Revenues Increase Y/Y" / Date: 2026-03-02]*; [Source: Seeking Alpha / Title: Vistra Corp. 2025 Q4 Results / Date: 2026-02-26]*

### Most Recent Full Year Report
For fiscal year 2025, Vistra generated operating revenues of $17.74 billion, up 3.0% from $17.22 billion in 2024. Net income was $944 million, down 66.4% from $2.81 billion in 2024. Net income attributable to Vistra common stock was $752 million after $192 million in cumulative preferred dividends, compared to $2.47 billion in 2024. The decline in reported net income was driven primarily by $808 million in non-cash unrealized mark-to-market losses on commodity hedges expected to settle in future years, as well as higher interest expense and impairment charges related to the Moss Landing battery incident. Cash flow from operations was $4.07 billion, down from $4.56 billion in 2024. [Source: Company 10-K / Title: FY2025 Annual Report / Date: 2026-02-26]***; [Source: Yahoo Finance / Title: VST Key Statistics / Date: 2026-05-01]**

Ongoing Operations Adjusted EBITDA reached a record $5.91 billion, up 4.8% from $5.64 billion in 2024 and above the original guidance midpoint by approximately $112 million. Ongoing Operations Adjusted Free Cash Flow before Growth (FCFbG) was $3.59 billion, exceeding guidance by roughly $292 million. [Source: Company Q4 2025 Earnings Release / Title: Q4 and Full-Year 2025 Results / Date: 2026-02-26]***

### Key Business Drivers in Recent Quarters
Vistra’s financial results are heavily influenced by three factors: (1) commodity price movements and the associated mark-to-market volatility on hedges; (2) the integration and contribution of acquired assets; and (3) the performance of its integrated retail-generation model.

In FY2025, the inclusion of a full year of Energy Harbor results (versus ten months in 2024) and two months of the Lotus gas portfolio contributed incremental EBITDA. Retail margins expanded due to favorable supply cost management and higher customer consumption driven by weather. These positives were partially offset by a $264 million reduction in nuclear Production Tax Credit (PTC) revenue in the East segment, higher operating costs from the inclusion of Energy Harbor, and $77 million in net operating costs related to the Moss Landing incident. [Source: Company 10-K / Title: FY2025 Annual Report / Date: 2026-02-26]***

Interest expense climbed to $1.18 billion in 2025 from $900 million in 2024, reflecting higher average borrowings related to the Vistra Vision noncontrolling interest buyout, the Lotus acquisition, and balance sheet leverage. [Source: Company 10-K / Title: FY2025 Annual Report / Date: 2026-02-26]***

### Segment Performance
Vistra reports through six segments: Retail, Texas, East, West, Corporate and Other, and Asset Closure.

**FY2025 Adjusted EBITDA by Segment:**
- **Retail:** $1,622 million (up from $1,463 million in 2024). Drivers included higher retail margins, customer count growth, and weather-driven consumption increases.
- **Texas:** $1,834 million (down from $2,032 million). The decline reflected lower nuclear PTC revenue, higher unrealized hedging losses, and outage impacts, partially offset by higher realized prices.
- **East:** $2,282 million (up from $2,017 million). The increase was driven by a full year of Energy Harbor ownership and higher realized energy and capacity prices, partially offset by the $264 million reduction in nuclear PTC revenue and higher unrealized mark-to-market losses.
- **West:** $244 million (up from $225 million).
- **Corporate and Other:** ($70 million) versus ($94 million).
- **Asset Closure:** ($74 million) versus ($104 million). The 2025 segment loss included a $155 million impairment of the Moss Landing 100 MW battery facility and remediation costs, partially offset by business interruption insurance proceeds. [Source: Company Q4 2025 Earnings Release / Title: Q4 and Full-Year 2025 Results / Date: 2026-02-26]***

### Geographic & FX Exposure
Vistra operates entirely within the United States across ERCOT, PJM, ISO-NE, CAISO, and other markets. It has no material foreign currency exposure. Its revenue and cost base are denominated in U.S. dollars, and commodity price exposure is primarily to domestic natural gas, power, and capacity markets.

## 2. Growth Trajectory

### Trajectory Assessment
Vistra’s growth trajectory over the past three years has been shaped by large-scale M&A and contracted revenue growth rather than organic greenfield expansion alone.

- **2023:** Revenue $14.78 billion; Ongoing Operations Adjusted EBITDA $4.09 billion.
- **2024:** Revenue $17.22 billion; Ongoing Operations Adjusted EBITDA $5.64 billion. The step-up was driven primarily by the Energy Harbor acquisition (closed March 2024).
- **2025:** Revenue $17.74 billion; Ongoing Operations Adjusted EBITDA $5.91 billion. Growth moderated as the Energy Harbor contribution annualized and retail margins improved, offset by lower nuclear PTCs and Moss Landing headwinds.
- **2026 Guidance:** Ongoing Operations Adjusted EBITDA of $6.8 billion to $7.6 billion (excluding any Cogentrix contribution), implying roughly 15% to 29% year-over-year growth at the midpoint.
- **2027 Midpoint Opportunity:** Management has maintained a $7.4 billion to $7.8 billion Ongoing Operations Adjusted EBITDA target, excluding Cogentrix and the Meta PPAs. [Source: Company Q4 2025 Earnings Release / Title: Q4 and Full-Year 2025 Results / Date: 2026-02-26]***; [Source: Seeking Alpha / Title: Vistra targets $6.8B–$7.6B adjusted EBITDA for 2026 / Date: 2025-11-06]*

The 2026–2027 growth outlook is anchored by:
- Multi-decade power purchase agreements (PPAs) with Amazon Web Services (AWS) for up to ~3,800 MW of nuclear power at Comanche Peak and PJM nuclear facilities.
- A 20-year PPA with Meta for more than 2,600 MW of energy, capacity, and uprates across PJM nuclear facilities, supporting subsequent license renewal applications.
- The pending Cogentrix acquisition (~5,500 MW of modern natural gas capacity), expected to close in mid-to-late 2026 and contribute to 2027 EBITDA.
- Continued growth in retail customer counts and business market volumes.

### Peer-Relative Growth Context
No comprehensive peer growth data was found in the search scope for direct comparison. However, Vistra’s projected 2026 Adjusted EBITDA growth of ~22% at the guidance midpoint appears to outpace traditional regulated utilities and reflects its merchant-plus-contracted model, which is currently benefiting from data-center-driven demand and scarcity pricing in constrained U.S. power markets. [Source: 24/7 Wall St. / Title: Vistra's 2026 Guidance Reinforces Its Multi-Year Growth Path / Date: 2026-02-26]*

## 3. Margin & Profitability Profile

**Metric Selection Justification:** For a vertically integrated power company with both retail and generation segments, traditional gross margin is distorted by pass-through fuel costs and volatile wholesale revenues. Management and the analyst community focus on **Adjusted EBITDA margin** and **segment-level Adjusted EBITDA** as the most meaningful profitability metrics. Net income is heavily influenced by non-cash unrealized hedge gains/losses and is therefore a less reliable operational indicator.

In FY2025, Ongoing Operations Adjusted EBITDA of $5.91 billion on $17.74 billion of revenue implies an adjusted EBITDA margin of approximately **33.3%**, down slightly from roughly 32.8% in 2024 on a pro forma basis but up materially from ~27.7% in 2023. The margin expansion reflects the higher-margin Energy Harbor nuclear assets, retail supply-cost management, and favorable pricing, partially offset by the loss of nuclear PTC revenue and higher operating costs.

**Profitability by segment (FY2025 Adjusted EBITDA contribution):**
- Retail: $1.62B (high-margin, stable)
- Texas Generation: $1.83B
- East Generation: $2.28B (largest contributor; includes nuclear fleet)
- West Generation: $0.24B
- Asset Closure: ($0.07B) drag

Reported operating income margin was 10.7% in 2025, down from 23.7% in 2024, reflecting the $808 million unrealized hedge loss and $228 million impairment charge. Net profit margin was 5.3% versus 16.3% in the prior year. [Source: Company 10-K / Title: FY2025 Annual Report / Date: 2026-02-26]***; [Source: Stock Titan / Title: VST Financials / Date: 2026-04-22]*

### Peer-Relative Profitability Context
Vistra’s Adjusted EBITDA margins appear above those of typical regulated utilities, reflecting its large-scale merchant generation and retail integration. However, direct margin comparisons with pure-play renewables or regulated utilities are of limited analytical value given Vistra’s hybrid model. No directly comparable peer margin data was sourced.

## 4. Cash Generation & Quality

### Cash Generation Quality Assessment
Vistra’s primary cash metric is **Adjusted Free Cash Flow before Growth (FCFbG)**, which management uses to evaluate the core cash-generating capacity of the existing fleet before growth capex and certain working capital swings.

For FY2025:
- Ongoing Operations Adjusted FCFbG: **$3.59 billion**
- Cash flow from operations: **$4.07 billion**
- Capital expenditures for maintenance (including nuclear fuel and LTSA prepayments): **$1.35 billion**
- Growth and development capex: **$1.13 billion** (excluded from FCFbG)
- Total capital expenditures: **$2.75 billion** [Source: Company Q4 2025 Earnings Release / Title: Q4 and Full-Year 2025 Results / Date: 2026-02-26]***

FCF conversion from Adjusted EBITDA to FCFbG was approximately **60.8%** ($3.59B / $5.91B), consistent with management’s historical target of 60%+. This suggests high-quality cash generation, though the absolute level of reported levered free cash flow is negative after growth capex and working capital changes. [Source: Zacks / Title: "Can VST Stock's Disciplined Capital Allocation Power Long-Term Growth?" / Date: 2025-10-24]*

### Working Capital & Cash Conversion
Working capital is **materially driven by margin deposits and collateral requirements** for commodity hedging rather than traditional receivables/payables dynamics. In FY2025, changes in working capital, margin deposits, and accrued environmental allowance obligations represented a **$625 million use of cash** within the FCFbG reconciliation. For the nine months ended September 30, 2025, net margin deposits related to commodity contracts were $361 million posted, compared to $855 million returned in the prior-year period, reflecting higher collateral demands as forward commodity prices moved. [Source: Company 10-K / Title: FY2025 Annual Report / Date: 2026-02-26]***; [Source: Company 10-Q / Title: Form 10-Q for Q3 2025 / Date: 2025-11-07]***

As of March 31, 2025, Vistra had posted $1.025 billion in cash and eligible assets with counterparties (up from $841 million at year-end 2024) and $2.439 billion in letters of credit. This collateral intensity means that liquidity can swing with commodity price volatility and clearing-agent margin requirements. [Source: Company 10-Q / Title: Form 10-Q for Q1 2025 / Date: 2025-05-02]***

### Earnings Quality
Reported GAAP earnings are of low quality as a near-term indicator due to large non-cash unrealized hedge marks. In FY2025, $808 million of unrealized pre-tax losses distorted GAAP net income while the economic value of the hedged generation improved. Conversely, nuclear PTC revenue ($145 million recognized in the first nine months of 2025) is a non-cash accounting benefit that supports earnings but does not immediately convert to cash unless monetized. Cash taxes paid in FY2025 were remarkably low at **$89 million**, reflecting the availability of nuclear PTCs, ITCs, and D&A shields. This is sustainable in the medium term but creates a deferred tax position. [Source: Company Q4 2025 Earnings Release / Title: Q4 and Full-Year 2025 Results / Date: 2026-02-26]***; [Source: Company 10-Q / Title: Form 10-Q for Q3 2025 / Date: 2025-11-07]***

## 5. Balance Sheet & Liquidity

As of December 31, 2025, Vistra reported total debt of approximately **$20.4 billion**, cash and equivalents of **$785 million**, and total available liquidity of roughly **$2.78 billion**. Total equity was approximately $5.58 billion, implying a total debt-to-equity ratio near 3.7x on a book basis and a current ratio of 0.78. [Source: Yahoo Finance / Title: VST Balance Sheet / Date: 2025-08-12 (data as of 2025-12-31)]**; [Source: Company Q4 2025 Earnings Release / Title: Q4 and Full-Year 2025 Results / Date: 2026-02-26]***

Net leverage (net debt / Ongoing Operations Adjusted EBITDA) was approximately **2.6x–3.0x** depending on calculation methodology, against management’s long-term target of less than 3.0x. [Source: Seeking Alpha / Title: Vistra's Growth Trajectory / Date: 2026-01-29]*; [Source: Investing.com / Title: Earnings Call Summary / Date: 2025 (unspecified)]*

### Debt Maturity Profile & Refinancing Risk
Vistra completed a **$4.0 billion private senior notes offering on April 22, 2026**, with proceeds used to repay or redeem existing indebtedness, specifically senior notes due February 2027 and a Term Loan B-3 facility, as well as for general corporate purposes. The offering consisted of four tranches:
- $500 million due 2028 at 4.550%
- $1.0 billion due 2031 at 5.000%
- $1.0 billion due 2033 at 5.250%
- $1.5 billion due 2036 at 5.550%

Net proceeds were approximately $3.97 billion. This refinancing directly addresses near-term maturity risk and extends the weighted average maturity of the debt stack. [Source: The Globe and Mail / Title: Vistra Corp Completes $4 Billion Senior Notes Offering / Date: 2026-04-30]***; [Source: TradingView / Title: Vistra Raises $4.0 Billion in Multi-Tranche Senior Notes / Date: 2026-04-29]***; [Source: Davis Polk / Title: Vistra $4 billion senior notes offering / Date: 2026-04-22]***

Vistra also issued secured notes in January 2026 (maturities 2031 and 2036), indicating active liability management. [Source: Sahm Capital / Title: Should Vistra's $4 Billion Debt Refinancing Shift Matter / Date: 2026-04-11]*

### Assessment
The balance sheet is highly leveraged but appears manageable against the company’s EBITDA generation and refinancing activity. The April 2026 transaction materially reduces refinancing risk for 2027. Key risks remain: (1) elevated absolute debt service ($1.2 billion guided interest expense for 2026); (2) potential collateral calls if commodity prices spike; and (3) the need to fund the Cogentrix acquisition with additional debt and equity. The current ratio below 1.0x is typical for utilities but relies on continuous capital market access.

## 6. Capital Deployment & Returns on Capital

### Share Repurchases
Since November 2021, Vistra has executed approximately **$5.9 billion** in share repurchases, reducing shares outstanding by roughly **30%** from ~482 million to ~337 million as of February 18, 2026. In FY2025, the company repurchased approximately $1.0 billion of stock. As of February 2026, roughly **$1.8 billion** remained under authorization, expected to be completed by year-end 2027. Management has guided to at least $1 billion of annual share repurchases. [Source: Company Q4 2025 Earnings Release / Title: Q4 and Full-Year 2025 Results / Date: 2026-02-26]***; [Source: TradingView / Title: Zacks commentary on capital allocation / Date: 2025-10-24]*

### Dividends
Vistra targets **$300 million annually** in common dividends. The quarterly dividend was raised to $0.2270 per share in Q4 2025 (annualized ~$0.91), reflecting steady but modest payout growth. The company also pays semi-annual dividends on three series of preferred stock (Series A, B, and C), with annualized rates of 8.0%, 7.0%, and 8.875%, respectively, on an aggregate liquidation preference that totaled at least $2.0 billion as of year-end 2023 and likely remains near $3.0 billion. Cumulative preferred dividends totaled **$192 million** in FY2025. [Source: Company Press Release / Title: Vistra Declares Dividend on Common and Preferred Stock / Date: 2025-10-30]***; [Source: SEC S-3ASR / Title: Vistra Shelf Registration / Date: 2024-01-26]***; [Source: Company 10-K / Title: FY2025 Annual Report / Date: 2026-02-26]***

### M&A / Capex
Capital deployment has been heavily skewed toward acquisitions:
- **Vistra Vision Noncontrolling Interest Buyout:** Closed December 31, 2024, for approximately **$3.2 billion** in cash, consolidating the 6.4 GW nuclear fleet and retail business. [Source: Yahoo Finance / Title: Vistra to become sole owner of Vistra Vision / Date: 2024-09-19]*; [Source: Company 10-K / Title: FY2025 Annual Report / Date: 2026-02-26]***
- **Lotus Acquisition:** Closed November 2025 for **$1.9 billion**, adding 2,600 MW of natural gas generation. [Source: Investing.com / Title: Vistra Q2 2025 Slides / Date: 2025-08-07]*; [Source: Financier Worldwide / Title: Vistra acquires Cogentrix / Date: 2026-01-08]*
- **Cogentrix Acquisition:** Announced January 2026, expected to close mid-to-late 2026. Net purchase price of approximately **$4.0 billion** ($2.3B cash, $0.9B stock at $185/share, $1.5B debt assumed, less ~$0.7B tax benefits), adding 5,500 MW. Goldman Sachs committed to provide up to ~$2 billion in bridge financing. [Source: Company Press Release / Title: Vistra Adds to Generation Portfolio with Acquisition of Cogentrix / Date: 2026-01-05]***; [Source: Utility Dive / Title: Vistra to buy Cogentrix / Date: 2026-01-06]*

**Capex breakdown FY2025:**
- Maintenance capex (including nuclear fuel/LTSA): $1.35 billion
- Growth and development capex: $1.13 billion
- Total capex: $2.75 billion [Source: Company Q4 2025 Earnings Release / Title: Q4 and Full-Year 2025 Results / Date: 2026-02-26]***

### Returns on Capital
Return on equity (ttm) was approximately **17.7%** as of early May 2026, but this figure is inflated by leverage and may overstate economic returns. Reported return on assets was 3.5%. Given the company’s capital-intensive nature, heavy depreciation, and recent M&A, traditional ROIC is difficult to interpret cleanly. A more meaningful lens is the **EBITDA-to-enterprise value yield** and **FCF conversion rate**. At an enterprise value of ~$74 billion and trailing EBITDA of ~$5.8–$5.9 billion, the EV/EBITDA multiple is roughly 12.5–14.7x depending on the EBITDA definition used. Management’s capital allocation appears focused on buying back stock when the EV/EBITDA multiple is perceived as undervaluing the fleet, and on accretive generation acquisitions (Cogentrix at ~7.25x 2027E EBITDA). [Source: Yahoo Finance / Title: VST Key Statistics / Date: 2026-05-01]**

## 7. Management Guidance & Forward Outlook

### Most Recent Full Year Report Guidance
For fiscal 2026, management provided the following guidance ranges for Ongoing Operations:
- **Adjusted EBITDA:** $6.8 billion to $7.6 billion
- **Adjusted FCFbG:** $3.925 billion to $4.725 billion

This guidance excludes any contribution from the Cogentrix assets. [Source: Company Q4 2025 Earnings Release / Title: Q4 and Full-Year 2025 Results / Date: 2026-02-26]***

### Most Recent Report Guidance
The Q4 2025 earnings release (February 2026) was the most recent comprehensive update. No Q1 2026 pre-announcement or guidance revision had been issued as of May 5, 2026.

### Management Commentary
CEO Jim Burke emphasized that 2025 was a “transformational year,” highlighting the AWS and Meta PPAs, the Lotus closing, and progress on solar and gas uprates. For 2026, management noted strong fleet performance during Winter Storm Fern and reiterated confidence in the structural power demand deficit, particularly from data centers. The 2027 Ongoing Operations Adjusted EBITDA midpoint opportunity was maintained at $7.4 billion to $7.8 billion, excluding Cogentrix and Meta. [Source: Company Q4 2025 Earnings Release / Title: Q4 and Full-Year 2025 Results / Date: 2026-02-26]***; [Source: Company Investor Presentation / Title: Q4 2025 Earnings Call Presentation / Date: 2026-02-26]***

### Forward Claim Confidence Weighting
- **2026 Adjusted EBITDA guidance ($6.8B–$7.6B): High confidence.** Approximately 100% of expected 2026 generation volumes were hedged as of February 18, 2026, providing substantial cash-flow visibility.
- **2026 Adjusted FCFbG guidance ($3.925B–$4.725B): High confidence.** Follows directly from EBITDA guidance and historically stable conversion rates.
- **2027 EBITDA midpoint opportunity ($7.4B–$7.8B): Medium confidence.** Depends on power price realization for unhedged volumes (84% hedged for 2027), retail execution, and no material asset outages.
- **Cogentrix closing timeline (mid-to-late 2026) and accretion: Medium confidence.** Subject to FERC, HSR, and state regulatory approvals.
- **Nuclear license renewal and PPA execution: Medium-to-High confidence.** The Meta PPA supports license renewal applications, but regulatory approval is not guaranteed.

## 8. Material Financial Risks & One-Off Items

**Moss Landing Battery Incident (January 16, 2025):** A fire at the 300 MW battery energy storage facility resulted in the write-off of approximately $400 million in net book value in Q1 2025. The 100 MW battery was subsequently impaired by $155 million in Q4 2025 and moved to Asset Closure; management determined it would not return to service. The 350 MW battery (NBV ~$320M) may return in mid-2026 pending investigation. Vistra entered an Administrative Settlement Agreement with the EPA estimating ~$110 million in remediation costs ($61 million accrued as of December 31, 2025). Additional litigation, regulatory, and reputational costs may follow. [Source: Company 10-K / Title: FY2025 Annual Report / Date: 2026-02-26]***; [Source: Fortune / Title: Form 10-K excerpt / Date: 2026-02-26]***

**Martin Lake Unit 1 Fire (November 27, 2024):** An 815 MW ERCOT unit was damaged and returned to service in February 2026. Restoration capex is estimated at ~$384 million, of which ~$271 million was incurred through year-end 2025. The majority of costs are expected to be recovered via property damage and business interruption insurance, but timing is uncertain. [Source: Company 10-K / Title: FY2025 Annual Report / Date: 2026-02-26]***

**Unrealized Commodity Hedge Volatility:** FY2025 GAAP net income included $808 million of non-cash unrealized losses from hedges. While these reverse as contracts settle, they create headline earnings volatility and can affect collateral requirements.

**Nuclear PTC Phase-Out Risk:** The IRA nuclear PTC is phased out as gross receipts rise. In FY2025, East segment nuclear PTC revenue declined by $264 million year-over-year. Further power price increases could reduce PTC eligibility, creating a partial offset to higher merchant revenues.

**Interest Rate and Refinancing Risk:** Interest expense rose 31% in 2025 to $1.18 billion. The April 2026 refinancing extended maturities but locked in coupons of 4.55%–5.55%. A sustained higher-rate environment would increase future refinancing costs.

**Regulatory and Litigation Exposure:** The Moss Landing incident has generated environmental liability, potential civil litigation, and reputational risk. The Cogentrix acquisition requires antitrust and FERC clearance.

## 9. Discrepancies vs. Structured Data Baseline

### Research Data vs. Stage 1 Baseline
**Stage 1 baseline not provided at runtime — section deferred.** No structured data baseline was supplied for reconciliation. All findings in this report are derived from primary filings, earnings releases, and financial data providers as of the research date.

## 10. Key Financial Assumptions Embedded in Current Valuation

As of May 5, 2026, Vistra’s stock traded near $155, implying a market capitalization of ~$52.6 billion and an enterprise value of ~$74.3 billion. The current valuation appears to embed the following financial assumptions:

1. **2026 Adjusted EBITDA approaching the upper half of guidance.** At a forward EV/EBITDA of roughly 10–11x on guided EBITDA, the market appears to price in execution toward $7.0B+ rather than the low end.
2. **Successful Cogentrix integration and immediate 2027 EBITDA accretion.** The deal multiple of ~7.25x implies value creation if the market awards Vistra a higher blended multiple, but execution risk is priced as modest.
3. **Sustained 60%+ FCF conversion.** The bull case depends on Vistra continuing to convert EBITDA to FCFbG at rates that cover ~$1.2B in interest, ~$300M in common dividends, and ~$240M in preferred dividends while still funding buybacks.
4. **Data-center demand durability.** The long-dated AWS and Meta PPAs are treated as high-certainty cash flows supporting nuclear asset valuations and license renewal economics.
5. **Hedge program effectiveness.** With 100% of 2026 generation hedged, the market assumes limited commodity downside, but also limited upside—making the 2026 guidance a fairly bounded outcome.
6. **No material Moss Landing liability escalation.** The current stock price likely assumes remediation costs remain near the ~$110M management estimate and that insurance covers most losses.

## 11. Bottom-Line Financial Assessment

### Business Model Characterization
Vistra operates as a **vertically integrated, capital-intensive power merchant with a contracted revenue overlay.** It combines a 41+ GW dispatchable generation fleet (nuclear, gas, coal, storage, solar) with a 5-million-customer retail platform. The business model generates value through: (1) low-cost baseload generation scarcity rents in constrained markets; (2) retail margin capture via supply-cost optimization; (3) long-duration contracted cash flows from hyperscale tech offtakers; and (4) financial engineering (aggressive buybacks, liability management). It is not a traditional regulated utility, nor is it a pure commodity merchant; rather, it is a hybrid “generation-plus-retail” platform with increasing reliance on contracted nuclear offtake.

### Strengths (confidence weighted)
- **Record operational EBITDA and high FCF conversion (High).** Ongoing Operations Adjusted EBITDA of $5.91B and FCFbG of $3.59B in 2025 demonstrate a cash-generative core.
- **Near-term earnings visibility from comprehensive hedging (High).** 100% of 2026 generation volumes were hedged as of February 2026, de-risking the near-term guidance.
- **Long-dated nuclear PPAs with creditworthy tech counterparties (High).** AWS and Meta contracts anchor decades of cash flow and support license renewal economics.
- **Disciplined capital return track record (Medium-High).** ~$5.9B in buybacks since 2021, a ~30% share count reduction, and a stated annual minimum of $1B in repurchases signal management alignment, though the pace depends on leverage and M&A priorities.
- **Strategic positioning for AI/data-center power demand (Medium).** The market narrative is supportive, and Vistra’s nuclear fleet is scarce; however, the financial impact is still in early stages.

### Weaknesses (confidence weighted)
- **Elevated leverage and interest burden (High).** Total debt of ~$20.4B and interest expense of $1.18B (guided ~$1.2B for 2026) consume a meaningful share of EBITDA. The debt maturity profile, while recently extended, remains aggressive for a merchant generator.
- **GAAP earnings volatility from hedge accounting (High).** $808M of unrealized losses in 2025 distorted reported earnings and will continue to create noise.
- **Moss Landing asset impairments and contingent liabilities (High).** ~$555M in combined write-downs ($400M + $155M), plus ~$110M estimated remediation and uncertain litigation exposure.
- **Nuclear PTC erosion if power prices rise (Medium).** The IRA credit phases out as gross receipts increase, creating a dampener on nuclear revenue upside that may not be fully appreciated in bullish scenarios.
- **Integration risk from Cogentrix and Lotus (Medium).** The company is absorbing ~8,100 MW of gas assets in less than 18 months while managing multiple outages and regulatory settlements.

### Watch Items
- **Q1 2026 earnings (to be reported May 7, 2026):** Look for commentary on Cogentrix regulatory approvals, Moss Landing 350 MW restart timing, and any change in 2026 hedge positioning.
- **Cogentrix closing and financing:** Monitor whether Vistra utilizes the full $2B bridge facility or pivots to alternative funding, and whether the deal closes on schedule.
- **Interest expense trajectory:** Despite refinancing, the April 2026 notes carry coupons up to 5.55%. Track whether 2026 interest expense can remain at or below the $1.2B guidance.
- **Collateral and liquidity:** Watch margin deposit trends in 10-Q filings. A spike in commodity prices could strain liquidity even if economically hedged.
- **Nuclear PTC guidance/IRS clarity:** Any Treasury regulation changes on gross receipts definitions could materially impact recognized revenue.
- **Moss Landing litigation and regulatory costs:** The $110M EPA estimate may prove conservative if additional hazardous waste is identified or civil suits proliferate.

## 12. Data Gaps & Limitations

1. **Stage 1 structured baseline:** Not provided at runtime; reconciliation in §9 was deferred.
2. **Q1 2026 financial results:** Not yet reported as of 2026-05-05 (scheduled for 2026-05-07). Analysis in §1 reflects Q4 2025 as the most recent quarter.
3. **Post-April 2026 pro forma balance sheet:** The $4.0B refinancing closed on April 22, 2026, but the updated debt maturity schedule and exact pro forma leverage were not available in filed 10-Q data as of the research date.
4. **Detailed peer-relative profitability metrics:** No directly comparable peer financial data (e.g., NRG, Calpine, Constellation) was sourced for margin or returns benchmarking.
5. **Exact FY2025 GAAP diluted EPS from primary filings:** The 10-K and earnings release did not prominently state diluted EPS; figures cited ($2.18–$2.34 for Q4; ~$2.17 ttm) are derived from third-party data providers.

# News & Narrative Deep Research: Vistra Corp (VST)

Research conducted: 2026-05-05

## 1. Most Significant News in the Window

**Q4 2025 Earnings and 2026 Guidance (Event date: February 26, 2026).** Vistra reported full-year 2025 ongoing operations adjusted EBITDA of $5.912 billion and ongoing operations adjusted free cash flow before growth of approximately $3.6 billion, both above the midpoint of original guidance. For 2026, management guided to ongoing operations adjusted EBITDA of $6.8 billion to $7.6 billion and adjusted free cash flow before growth of $3.925 billion to $4.725 billion, excluding any Cogentrix contribution. The stock rose roughly 1% in premarket trading following the release, suggesting the results were largely anticipated rather than revelatory. [Source: Company press release / Title: "Vistra Reports Fourth Quarter and Full-Year 2025 Results" / Date: 2026-02-26] ***

**Nuclear Power Purchase Agreements with Meta and Amazon (Announced January 2026).** On January 9, 2026, Vistra announced a 20-year PPA with Meta covering more than 2,600 MW of nuclear energy, capacity, and uprates across its PJM nuclear fleet. This followed a previously disclosed 20-year agreement with Amazon Web Services for up to 1,200 MW from the Comanche Peak nuclear plant. The Meta deal is notable for directly underwriting nuclear uprates and supporting subsequent license renewal for all four PJM nuclear units. Together, these contracts are central to the bull thesis, providing long-duration contracted revenue and underpinning the nuclear fleet's extended life. [Source: Carbon Credits / Title: "Vistra Leverages Nuclear Partnerships with Meta and Amazon to Drive 2026 Growth" / Date: 2026-03-09] *; [Source: Company press release / Title: "Vistra Reports Fourth Quarter and Full-Year 2025 Results" / Date: 2026-02-26] ***

**Cogentrix Energy Acquisition (Announced January 5, 2026).** Vistra agreed to acquire Cogentrix Energy for approximately $4 billion in cash and stock, adding roughly 5,500 MW of modern natural gas-fired capacity across PJM, ISO-New England, and ERCOT. The deal is expected to close in mid-to-late 2026 and follows the November 2025 closing of the $1.9 billion Lotus Infrastructure Partners acquisition (2,600 MW). Management has framed the Cogentrix deal as highly accretive and critical to meeting surging data-center-driven load growth in the Northeast and Mid-Atlantic. [Source: POWER Magazine / Title: "Vistra to Bolster Gas-Fired Fleet by 5.5 GW with $4B Cogentrix Acquisition" / Date: 2026-01-14] **; [Source: Natural Gas Intel / Title: "Vistra Builds Natural Gas Power Capacity Spanning ERCOT, ISO-NE and PJM" / Date: 2026-01-12] **

**Achievement of Investment-Grade Credit Ratings (March 17, 2026).** Fitch upgraded Vistra's long-term issuer default rating to BBB-, following S&P Global's upgrade to investment grade in December 2025. The company cited "sustained free cash flow generation," "disciplined capital allocation," and "increased visibility and stability in earnings profile, supported by the recently announced long-term generation power purchase agreements with Amazon and Meta" as drivers. [Source: Company press release / Title: "Vistra Achieves Investment-Grade Credit Ratings from S&P and Fitch" / Date: 2026-03-17] ***; [Source: Fitch Ratings / Title: "Fitch Upgrades Vistra and Vistra Ops to 'BBB-'; Outlook Stable" / Date: 2026-03-16] **

**$4 Billion Senior Notes Offering (Completed April 22, 2026).** Vistra swiftly monetized its new investment-grade status by pricing and completing a $4.0 billion private offering of senior notes in late April 2026. The offering was disclosed in an 8-K filing dated April 28, 2026. [Source: StockInsights 8-K summary / Title: "VST VISTRA CORP Capital Structure Changes 8-K Filing" / Date: 2026-04-28] ***

**Significant Stock Decline and Analyst Price Target Cuts (Late April 2026).** Despite the positive fundamental developments, Vistra's stock declined approximately 25% from its fall 2025 highs, trading in the $155–$161 range in late April and early May 2026 versus a 52-week high of roughly $220. In the final days of April 2026, multiple sell-side firms cut price targets while maintaining positive ratings: TD Cowen lowered its target from $253 to $230 (May 4); JPMorgan from $240 to $231 (April 30); Raymond James from $240 to $208 (April 27); Morgan Stanley from $214 to $208 (April 20); and Wells Fargo from $236 to $234. [Source: TipRanks / Title: VST Stock Forecast / Date: 2026-05-04] *; [Source: Motley Fool / Title: "The Crowd Is Dumping Vistra. Here's Why I'd Be Buying the Stock Down 25%." / Date: 2026-04-18] *

**Upcoming Q1 2026 Earnings (Scheduled May 7, 2026).** Vistra is scheduled to report Q1 2026 results before the market opens on May 7, 2026, with a conference call at 10:00 AM ET. Consensus estimates vary by aggregator, with Zacks forecasting EPS of $1.39 on revenue of $5.41 billion (representing +202% YoY EPS growth), while other sources cite consensus EPS near $1.32 on revenue of approximately $5.15 billion. Notably, the Zacks consensus EPS estimate for Q1 was revised 3.33% lower over the preceding 30 days. [Source: Yahoo Finance / Title: "Vistra Corp. (VST) Earnings Expected to Grow: What to Know Ahead of Next Week's Release" / Date: 2026-05-01] *; [Source: MarketBeat / Title: "Vistra Q1 2026 Earnings Report" / Date: 2026-05-04] *

---

## 2. Earnings Call Deep Dive

*(Q4 2025 and Full-Year 2025 Earnings Call, held February 26, 2026)*

### Headline Takeaways

Management delivered a confident, execution-focused message centered on "transformational" strategic progress and record financial performance. The dominant themes were: (1) the durability of load growth driven by AI/data centers, (2) the stabilizing effect of long-term nuclear PPAs, and (3) disciplined capital allocation supporting both growth and shareholder returns. However, the CFO explicitly flagged that 2025 retail results included non-recurring tailwinds, tempering expectations for that segment.

### Management Tone & Conviction

CEO Jim Burke opened with assertive, upbeat language: "2025 was a transformational year for Vistra Corp. We made a number of moves that I believe underscore the value of our integrated model." He emphasized that the company "delivered another year of record financial performance" and expressed conviction in "the ever-increasing customer demand for power." [Source: Motley Fool transcript / Title: "Vistra (VST) Q4 2025 Earnings Call Transcript" / Date: 2026-02-26] **

Burke was explicit about the demand outlook: "Our primary regions continue to outperform. We maintain our view that annual peak load growth of at least 3% to 5% in ERCOT and low single-digit growth in PJM is achievable through 2030." He also noted that "data center development remains robust" and cited hyperscaler capex expected to "eclipse $700,000,000,000 in 2026, equivalent to roughly 50% year-over-year growth." [Source: Motley Fool transcript / Date: 2026-02-26] **

CFO Kris Moldovan struck a slightly more measured tone on the retail segment, stating: "Although the retail business continues to generate strong earnings in a variety of market conditions, 2025's record result was partly driven by some tailwinds that are not expected to repeat in the future, including some supply cost benefits and gains related to the Energy Harbor acquisition. Over the medium term, we continue to expect retail to achieve adjusted EBITDA in the neighborhood of approximately $1.4 billion." [Source: MarketBeat transcript excerpt / Date: 2026-02-26] *

Notably, Burke disclosed during his closing remarks that he would "be unable to join the live Q&A portion due to an unforeseen personal matter." While he emphasized confidence in his team's ability to handle questions, the absence of the CEO from the live Q&A session was an unusual element of the call. [Source: Motley Fool transcript / Date: 2026-02-26] **

### Q&A Pushback & Analyst Skepticism

Analysts pressed on three main areas: (1) the impact of PJM market rule changes on the Meta deal and future colocation opportunities; (2) the normalization of retail earnings; and (3) the timing and structure of future hyperscaler contracts.

On PJM rules, management pushed back against concerns, clarifying that the Meta PPA is a "typical front-of-the-meter deal not tied to colocation or any particular load" and therefore not directly affected by PJM's co-location tariff reforms. However, management acknowledged that "clarity on colocation tariff provisions could facilitate discussions about Beaver Valley and other colocation opportunities," implying that regulatory uncertainty is a gating item for future deals. [Source: Intellectia AI earnings summary / Date: 2026-02-26] (lower-credibility summary; no primary transcript source available for full Q&A exchange)

On capital allocation, analysts questioned why the 2027 midpoint opportunity had not yet been updated to include the Meta deal. Management responded that it would update guidance after the Cogentrix deal closes, and noted that the Meta contract could add $700–$750 million to 2027 EBITDA, but with full contributions from the second Meta contract only beginning in late 2027. This appeared to create some near-term disappointment among investors hoping for immediate upward revisions. [Source: Intellectia AI earnings summary / Date: 2026-02-26]

### Forward Commentary & Guidance

Management guided 2026 ongoing operations adjusted EBITDA to $6.8 billion–$7.6 billion and adjusted free cash flow before growth to $3.925 billion–$4.725 billion, both excluding Cogentrix. They projected generating "more than $10 billion of cash through year-end 2027," with approximately $3 billion allocated to equity holders (share repurchases and dividends) and approximately $4 billion toward accretive growth investments, including Cogentrix, Permian gas units, and PJM nuclear upgrades. Even after these allocations, management expects "more than $3 billion of additional capital available to allocate through year-end 2027," while achieving a net debt to adjusted EBITDA ratio of approximately 2.3x by year-end 2027. [Source: MarketBeat transcript excerpt / Date: 2026-02-26] *

Analyst Estimates / Date: unknown] *; [Source: TipRanks / Title: VST Stock Forecast / Date: 2026-05-04] *

## 4. Regulatory, Legal & Compliance Developments

**FERC and PJM Co-Location/Colocation Rules (Ongoing).** In January 2026, FERC ordered PJM to reform its tariff for co-located generation and load, establishing a paper hearing to determine rates, terms, and conditions for new transmission services. PJM was directed to file compliance revisions by mid-February 2026, with responses due March 18, 2026, and replies due April 17, 2026. While Vistra management has stated that its existing Meta PPA is a front-of-the-meter deal unaffected by these rules, the regulatory backdrop has clearly spooked investors concerned that future colocation opportunities—and the economics of hyperscaler deals—could be constrained or made more costly. [Source: K&L Gates / Title: "FERC Orders PJM to Reform Tariff for Co-Located Generation and Load" / Date: 2026-01-15] **; [Source: Mayer Brown / Title: "FERC Directs PJM to Facilitate Co-Location Arrangements" / Date: 2026-01-13] **

**PJM Capacity Market Price Caps/Collars.** FERC previously approved price caps and collars for PJM Base Residual Auctions for delivery years 2026/27 and 2027/28, with a cap of $325/MW-day and a floor of $175/MW-day. The 13 PJM governors have sought to extend these collars for the 2028/29 and 2029/30 auctions. This has emerged as a material bear narrative, with concerns that wholesale sellers like Vistra will be unable to capture upside in tight capacity markets. [Source: Diversegy / Title: "FERC Approves PJM Capacity Cap & Collar for 2026–2028" / Date: 2025-08-11] *; [Source: Motley Fool / Title: "The Crowd Is Dumping Vistra..." / Date: 2026-04-18] *

**Winter Storm Uri Litigation (Ongoing).** A multi-district litigation in Texas concerning generator performance during Winter Storm Uri remains pending. In December 2023, the First Court of Appeals granted a mandamus petition instructing the MDL court to grant motions to dismiss for generator defendants; plaintiffs have petitioned the Texas Supreme Court to review. [Source: SEC filing (Form 10-K excerpt) / Date: 2020-02-12 URL but content reflects current disclosures] *** (Note: Citation relies on SEC filing text; URL date is archive-related)

**FERC Settlement Re: Dynegy/MISO Capacity Auction.** On July 30, 2025, the parties filed a $38 million settlement agreement with FERC to resolve allegations related to a 2015–16 MISO capacity auction involving pre-Vistra Dynegy assets. The company also declared a quarterly common dividend of $0.229 per share in late April 2026, a modest ~0.4% sequential increase payable June 30. As of February 2026, Vistra had spent $200 million year-to-date on share repurchases under its 10b5-1 plan, which management noted is "structured to be more active when share prices are under pressure." [Source: MarketBeat / Date: 2026-05-02] *; [Source: MarketBeat transcript excerpt / Date: 2026-02-26] *

The market appears to be reading capital returns as a signal of cash-flow confidence, though some commentary suggests buybacks could be viewed as a substitute for organic growth if deal flow slows.

### Material Insider Transactions

Insider selling has been persistent and one-sided over the past six months. According to Quiver Quantitative data, Vistra insiders executed 48 open-market sales and zero purchases over the prior six months. CEO James A. Burke sold 662,707 shares for an estimated $133.8 million. Other executives including Carrie Lee Kirby (EVP and Chief Admin. Officer), Scott A. ## 6. Sentiment & Positioning

### Institutional Sentiment

Institutional ownership stands at approximately 90.88%. Specific Q1 2026 13F filing data was not available as of the research date (funds have not yet filed for the March 31 quarter). HedgeFollow data shows no major initiations or exits filed in the most recent Q4 2025 reporting window. No material 13D activist filings were identified. [Source: MarketBeat / Title: Vistra Institutional Ownership / Date: 2026-05-04] *

### Retail & Social Sentiment

Retail sentiment appears mixed to negative in the wake of the ~25% stock decline. Motley Fool commentary characterized retail investors as "dumping" the stock in mid-April 2026. Social media and financial press coverage has focused on the regulatory overhang narrative. This is marked as lower-signal institutional noise unless it amplifies into broader sentiment shifts. [Source: Motley Fool / Title: "The Crowd Is Dumping Vistra..." / Date: 2026-04-18] *

### Short Interest

Short interest totaled 11,237,560 shares as of April 15, 2026, a 20.4% increase from the March 31, 2026 total of 9,333,104 shares. Approximately 3.4% of the company's shares are sold short, with a days-to-cover ratio of 3.3 based on average trading volume of 3,392,087 shares. The upward trend in short interest suggests growing bearish positioning ahead of the Q1 2026 earnings report. Most recent as of: 2026-04-15. [Source: MarketBeat / Title: "Short Interest in Vistra Corp. (NYSE:VST) Rises By 20.4%" / Date: 2026-04-30] *

### Valuation Narrative

The dominant valuation debate centers on multiple compression. Vistra's forward P/E has fallen from approximately 37.6x in fall 2025 to roughly 19.3x as of mid-April 2026. Bulls argue the stock has "de-risked" and trades at a significant discount to analyst consensus targets (~$230–$235), with long-term contracted cash flows from Meta and Amazon providing earnings stability. Bears argue the compression is warranted given regulatory risks (PJM price caps, FERC colocation scrutiny) and the potential for retail earnings normalization to undercut 2025's record results. The market appears to be weighing whether AI-driven power demand is sufficiently "locked in" via contracts, or whether political and regulatory headwinds will cap the upside. [Source: Motley Fool / Title: "The Crowd Is Dumping Vistra..." / Date: 2026-04-18] *; [Source: Yahoo Finance / Title: "Vistra's Data Center Power Deals And Acquisitions Leave Valuation Question Open" / Date: 2026-03-03] *

---

## 7. Short-Seller Research & Activist Investor Involvement

No short-seller reports or activist investor activity identified in the research window. Short interest has risen mechanically, but no prominent short-seller has publicly issued a research report targeting Vistra. No 13D activist filings were located.

---

## 8. Narrative Shifts

**A clear narrative shift has occurred.**

- **Prior narrative (late 2025):** Vistra was framed as a premier AI-power beneficiary and nuclear renaissance play. The stock had risen approximately 7x over three years. Investor focus was on accelerating data-center load growth, the scarcity of 24/7 carbon-free baseload power, and multiple expansion driven by long-duration contracted revenue.
- **Current narrative (early 2026):** The narrative has shifted to a "regulatory overhang / multiple compression" story. While the long-term demand thesis is still acknowledged, investor attention has pivoted toward risks: PJM capacity price caps, FERC scrutiny of colocation deals, potential cost-shifting to residential customers, and retail earnings normalization. The stock has derated sharply despite a stream of positive fundamental news (investment-grade ratings, nuclear PPAs, strong guidance).
- **Trigger:** The shift was triggered by a combination of (1) the January 2026 FERC order on PJM co-location tariffs, which raised fears that hyperscaler deal economics could be regulated; (2) the late-2025/early-2026 implementation of PJM capacity market price collars; (3) a broader rotation out of higher-volatility utility/power names; and (4) CFO commentary on the February 2026 earnings call flagging non-recurring retail tailwinds.

---

## 9. Narrative-vs-Reality Disconnects

**A material disconnect appears to exist.**

The dominant market narrative in early 2026 is cautious-to-bearish, emphasizing regulatory caps and multiple compression. However, this pessimism is sitting on top of several observable positive realities: Vistra achieved investment-grade ratings from both S&P and Fitch for the first time in its history; it has secured 20-year contracted revenue with two of the world's largest tech companies; it guided to strong 2026 EBITDA and free cash flow; and it is actively deploying capital into accretive gas and nuclear uprate projects. The stock trades at a forward P/E roughly half its peak level, despite these fundamental improvements.

Conversely, a secondary disconnect may exist on the bull side: long-term nuclear PPAs and AI demand are priced as near-certainties, while the regulatory process around PJM cost allocation, FERC colocation rules, and potential political backlash against data-center-driven rate increases remains unresolved. The bull narrative may be underpricing the execution and regulatory risk embedded in converting announced projects into realized returns.

---

## 10. Bottom-Line Narrative Assessment

### Narrative Characterization

As of May 2026, Vistra is caught between two competing stories. The bulls see a transformed, investment-grade power generator with decades of contracted cash flow from tech giants, massive exposure to structurally higher power demand, and a derated valuation. The bears see a stock still vulnerable to regulatory intervention in PJM, retail earnings normalization, and the risk that AI demand fails to materialize as quickly as priced. The prevailing wind is currently blowing toward the bears, as evidenced by a 25%+ drawdown, rising short interest, and a wave of analyst target cuts.

### Key Narrative Drivers (confidence weighted)

1. **AI/Data-center demand and nuclear PPA monetization (High confidence).** The Meta and Amazon deals are real, long-term, and directly supportive of nuclear uprates. This is the core load-bearing bull driver.
2. **Regulatory overhang in PJM and FERC colocation scrutiny (High confidence).** This is the core load-bearing bear driver. The rules are actively being written, and the uncertainty is measurably compressing multiples.
3. **Retail earnings normalization post-2025 record (Medium confidence).** Management explicitly flagged this. It is well-evidenced but already partially priced in.
4. **Cogentrix acquisition execution and financing (Medium confidence).** The deal is announced and expected to close mid-to-late 2026, but integration and regulatory approval risks remain.
5. **Investment-grade ratings and balance sheet strength (Medium confidence).** Achieved, but the market is debating whether this translates to sustained multiple expansion or merely supports deleveraging.

### Narrative Risks (confidence weighted)

1. **Adverse FERC/PJM rulemaking materially impairs hyperscaler deal economics (Medium confidence).** If colocation or transmission cost allocations shift unfavorably, Vistra's growth of high-margin contracted revenue could slow.
2. **Q1 2026 earnings miss or guidance tweak confirming retail weakness (Medium confidence).** Estimate revisions have already trended lower; a miss could accelerate the bear narrative.
3. **Cogentrix deal closure delayed or terms renegotiated (Low confidence).** No specific rumors identified, but M&A execution is always a risk.
4. **Broader sector rotation away from power/AI infrastructure (Medium confidence).** The stock has shown high correlation with sector moves; macro rotations can override fundamentals.
5. **Nuclear operational issues or license renewal delays (Low confidence).** No specific developments identified, but the nuclear fleet is central to the thesis.

### Catalysts to Watch

- **Q1 2026 Earnings Release and Call:** May 7, 2026, before market open; call at 10:00 AM ET. Key focus: retail segment performance, hedge positions, and any update on Cogentrix timing or data-center contracting.
- **Cogentrix Acquisition Closing:** Expected mid-to-late 2026. Exact date TBD; watch for HSR/DOJ and FERC approvals.
- **PJM/FERC Colocation Rule Finalization:** Paper hearing replies were due April 17, 2026; a FERC order or PJM compliance filing could emerge in Q2/Q3 2026.
- **Texas Supreme Court Decision:** Pending review of Winter Storm Uri MDL generator dismissals. Timing uncertain but could resolve a long-tail liability.
- **FERC Approval of Dynegy Settlement:** $38 million settlement pending; approval could remove an overhang.
- **Next Quarterly Dividend Declaration:** Late July/early August 2026 (implied by quarterly cadence).

---

## 11. Data Gaps & Limitations

- **Detailed Q1 2026 13F institutional flow data:** Not yet filed for the quarter ended March 31, 2026. Search scope: SEC EDGAR, WhaleWisdom, HedgeFollow. Analytical impact: inability to identify whether the late-April price target cuts coincided with institutional selling.
- **Complete primary transcript of Q4 2025 earnings call Q&A:** Only excerpts and AI-generated summaries were accessible. Search scope: Seeking Alpha (paywalled), Motley Fool (partial), GuruFocus. Analytical impact: Q&A pushback characterization relies on secondary summaries rather than direct analyst quotes.
- **Specific dollar terms of Meta and Amazon nuclear PPAs:** The contracts are described in megawatts and duration, but pricing ($/MWh) has not been publicly disclosed. Search scope: Company press releases, SEC filings, trade press. Analytical impact: limits precision of revenue/cash flow modeling.
- **Retail sentiment/social media volume metrics:** No dedicated social sentiment data provider (e.g., Reddit trend scores, StockTwits volume) was accessed. Search scope: general web search. Analytical impact: retail sentiment assessment is qualitative only.
- **Detailed options positioning (strike clustering, put/call skew):** Only general directional flow notes (e.g., "call volume above normal" per TipRanks) were available. Search scope: web search. Analytical impact: limits granularity of institutional sentiment assessment.
